copy: $SPY is grinding higher and nobody believes it. Sellers keep trying to fade the move but buyers keep absorbing every dip. That's how squeezes build. The model caught it.
Credit markets are quietly reopening for risk while everyone is still positioned for a crash. The gap between what bonds are saying and what Twitter is saying is where moves start.
Why are you still trading 15m RSI? It tells you what already happened. Comparing today to 10 years of similar sessions tells you what usually happens next. One is lag, the other is context.
Semis are leading again. $NVDA and $SOXX are catching a bid while everyone is calling the top too early. When growth starts outperforming, fighting it usually costs money.
Why are you still trading 15m RSI? Momentum signals fire after the move. Pattern data frames probability before anyone else sees it. One is reactive. The other is context. That's the edge.

Oil just moved and nobody's talking about it. $USO is quietly repricing inflation risk while everyone watches $SPY. The model caught this shift days ago. Most traders won't notice until it's too late.
Money is quietly moving into utilities ($XLU) while the indexes look fine on the surface. When the big money starts hiding before anything breaks, that's usually worth paying attention to.

Yesterday the model said risk-on. $SPY gapped up and never looked back. Traders who faded the open got smoked. The data had the answer before the bell even rang. That's what 10 years of pattern matching does.
The dollar ($DXY) is weakening and that's usually good for growth stocks. The model flipped to this read two days ago. Most of Twitter still hasn't noticed.
VIX structure just changed. The big players stopped pushing for more upside. Meanwhile everyone on here is still calling for breakout. That disconnect usually ends badly for late chasers.
$NVDA led. $QQQ followed. The model said growth would hold as long as credit stayed open. It did. Dip buyers won. Everyone waiting for a "cleaner entry" is still waiting.
Everyone sees $SPY chopping sideways and gets bored. But underneath, money is rotating into new sectors. The index looks dead. The internals look like a setup. The model sees it. Do you?
$QQQ dipped and got bought right back. Bears pressed hard for follow-through and got nothing. That's not weakness, that's a failed breakdown. The model saw this coming.

RSI overbought doesn't mean sell. $SPY has gone overbought and kept running for weeks during strong trends. The only way to know if it's early or late is comparing today to similar setups from the past 10 years.

Indicators tell you what price already did. Pattern matching tells you what price usually does next.

That's the difference between reacting and anticipating. One of those makes money consistently.

The model tagged a low-vol squeeze setup before $SPY broke higher. Half of Twitter was shorting resistance. The pattern said squeeze. It squeezed. This is why you check the data before you take the trade.

First red candle of the rally and everyone panicked. The model said don't fear it, this pattern almost always recovers. $QQQ was green by lunch. Sometimes the best trade is just not selling the shakeout.

The market doesn't care about your favorite indicator. It rewards you for knowing when conditions are dangerous vs. when they're normal.

That's what 10 years of pattern data gives you. Context your chart can't.

Credit held. Volatility stayed low. $SPY kept grinding higher. The model said continuation, not crash. That's exactly what happened. Bears have been calling the top for three weeks and losing money the whole time.
The model flagged semis before the move hit $QQQ. By the time Twitter saw it, the easy money was made. That's the problem with trading what already happened instead of what usually happens next.
$IWM is diverging from $SPY. Small caps are quietly getting accumulated while the index goes nowhere. Most people tune out when the tape gets boring. That's usually where the next move starts.
Gold ($GLD) is accelerating while stocks are still going up. That doesn't usually last. Someone big is buying protection while still riding equities. Worth watching closely.
$QQQ looks strong but zoom in. It's being carried by like 3 names. That's not a healthy trend, that's a house of cards. When those names rotate, the whole index goes with them.
Bonds ($TLT) are getting bid while stocks are still green. The big money is quietly buying insurance. Surface says bullish. Bond market says be careful. I trust the bond market.
Energy ($XLE) is waking up while $SPY goes nowhere. Nobody's paying attention to sector rotation right now. That's exactly when it works.

"RSI is overbought so I'm shorting."

How many times has that cost you money? Overbought can stay overbought for weeks in a strong market. The only way to know is to compare today's setup to what actually happened in similar ones.

Every morning before the bell I send a free email.

It tells you one thing: is the market risk-on, risk-off, or neutral today? Based on 9 ETFs and 10 years of data, not vibes.

90-second read. Might save you from your worst trade of the day.

Every trader thinks their chart is unique. But the market has seen this tape before, hundreds of times. The question isn't what your indicator says. It's what happened next on the days that looked like this one.

+41,576% long-only vs +941% BTC buy-and-hold.

Funding rates spiking + open interest rising + regime score negative. The model has seen this pattern 47 times since 2020. It faded 39 of them. Knowing when to sit out is the edge nobody talks about.

A $10,000 BTC position on Jan 1, 2020 using our regime model is worth over $4.1 million today.

DXY rallying + BTC holding. That divergence doesn't last. Either the dollar rolls or BTC catches down. The regime model is already positioned. Are you?

The hardest part of trading crypto isn't finding entries. It's knowing which days to skip.

Our regime model sat out 40% of sessions since 2020. The ones it skipped averaged -0.8%. The ones it traded averaged +1.2%.

That gap, compounded daily, is the difference between +941% and +41,576%.

You're reading CT threads, checking funding rates, watching whale wallets, refreshing CoinGecko.

What if one email compressed all of that into a single daily score — risk-on or risk-off — with the historical hit rate attached?

That's what we built.

Everyone is watching $BTC at resistance while the real story is what credit markets are doing underneath. Crypto doesn't trade in a vacuum. The macro context matters more than the chart.
Honest question for traders: do you check any macro data before the open, or just look at $SPY and go? Curious what your process looks like.
Dollar weakness and $BTC strength at the same time. That's not a coincidence, that's a regime. The crypto model scores these cross-asset signals daily. Free briefing, 90 seconds, before you trade.
The model flagged risk-off two days before the selloff started. Most traders were still buying dips. You can see the full track record, including the misses.

Altcoins are bleeding while $BTC holds. That's classic risk-off behaviour inside crypto. When capital concentrates into one asset, the regime is telling you something. The model quantifies it daily.
Three risk-on calls in a row, all correct. The model doesn't streak often but when it does, ignoring the signal gets expensive. Full history, no cherry picking.
What's the one indicator you can't trade without? Not what sounds smart. The one you actually use every single day.
The $BTC funding rate just flipped negative while price is flat. Last 5 times that happened, the resolution was up within 3 days. The model tracks these setups across 6 years of data.
Every morning before you trade: is the market risk-on, risk-off, or neutral today? That one question changes how you size, where you look, and when you sit out. 90 seconds. Free.

Quick poll: when bonds and equities disagree, which one do you trust more? The answer says a lot about how you trade.
The dollar is catching a bid and nobody is adjusting their equity exposure for it. When DXY moves and $SPY doesn't react, one of them is wrong. The model checks this every morning.
The crypto model avoided the worst of the last drawdown by going to cash before the drop. Not prediction. Pattern matching. The regime score turned negative 2 days before the move.
The market has five distinct regimes. Extreme risk-on, risk-on, neutral, risk-off, extreme risk-off. Each one has a different playbook. Trading the wrong playbook is how most losses happen.

$VIX is compressing while breadth is narrowing. That combination has led to sharp moves in either direction. The question isn't if, it's when. The model matched today to 42 similar sessions.
Small caps are quietly outperforming for the first time in weeks. $IWM leading $SPY is usually early confirmation of risk appetite returning. Most traders won't see it until the move is done.
How many of you actually track cross-asset correlation before the bell? Or do you just wing it based on what FinTwit is saying?
Credit is tightening while the indexes sit at highs. HYG/TLT is rolling over. That divergence has preceded every meaningful pullback in the last 3 years. The model weighs this every single day.
You don't need 15 indicators. You need one question answered every morning: what is the market actually doing right now, and what did similar days lead to? The briefing answers that in 90 seconds.

Gold just broke out and nobody on FinTwit is talking about it. $GLD catching a bid while equities chop usually means real money is hedging something. Worth knowing before you go long at the open.
Energy is leading again. When $USO and $XLE start moving together while tech stalls, the rotation is telling you something about where real money is going. The model catches these shifts daily.
Crypto traders: do you check what equities are doing before you size a $BTC position? Or does macro not matter to you?
FinTwit consensus is bearish. The model is neutral. When everyone agrees on the direction, the data usually disagrees. That's not contrarian for the sake of it. That's pattern matching.

Most crypto traders are using stock market tools on a 24/7 market. RSI, MACD, Bollinger Bands. The crypto model was built for crypto volatility from the ground up. Different asset class, different approach.
Everyone is calling for a crash. The model matched today to 39 similar sessions. 28 of them were green the next day. Not a guarantee, but worth knowing before you press the sell button.
The best trades often feel wrong at the open. When the model says risk-on and your gut says sell, that tension is where the edge lives. Data beats opinion, every time.
If you trade $BTC without checking what the dollar, credit, and equities are doing, you're trading with one eye closed. Crypto doesn't move in isolation. The macro context is the edge.
